summary.title: Dataspace Control Plane — Platform Threat Model
summary.description: Platform-wide DFD covering the main trust boundaries between control-api, temporal-workers, Vault, Keycloak, Postgres, and external connectors.

data.name: Operator
data.description: Human operator using web-console to manage companies, passports, and workflows

data.name: control-api
data.description: FastAPI HTTP service handling operator and machine requests. Validates JWTs, enforces role-based access, forwards long-running operations to Temporal.

threats.title: JWT token replay
threats.description: Attacker replays a captured JWT to impersonate an operator after the operator has logged out or the token should have expired
threats.mitigation: Short-lived JWTs (5 min expiry) validated server-side via JWKS endpoint on every request. MFA enforced. Keycloak session revocation available.

threats.title: Injection via ODRL policy payload
threats.description: Malformed ODRL policy payload from an external EDC connector may cause undefined behavior in the Catena-X pack policy compiler, potentially allowing policy bypass or application crash
threats.mitigation: Validate against odrl.policy-offer schema before processing. Hypothesis property tests cover parser inputs. Fuzz testing of all constraint combinations is pending (Wave 3 hardening backlog).

threats.title: Rate limit bypass via forged tenant ID
threats.description: Attacker attempts to bypass per-tenant rate limits by cycling through tenant IDs
threats.mitigation: Rate limits applied per JWT sub claim (user ID), not per tenant ID header. A stolen JWT from one tenant cannot be used to affect another tenant's rate limit.

data.name: PostgreSQL
data.description: Tenant-isolated data store with Row-Level Security policies on all tenant tables. Stores companies, agreements, passports, evidence records (append-only), and metering contexts.

threats.title: Cross-tenant query via forged tenant ID
threats.description: Attacker forges X-Tenant-ID header to access another tenant's data. Even if the API-layer filter is misconfigured, PostgreSQL RLS is the final enforcement layer.
threats.mitigation: PostgreSQL RLS policies enforce tenant isolation at the database layer. The app.tenant_id session variable is set from the validated JWT claim, not from request headers. Forged header value does not affect the session variable.

threats.title: Evidence record tampering via SQL UPDATE
threats.description: Attacker with compromised application credentials attempts to UPDATE an evidence record to falsify the audit trail
threats.mitigation: The dataspace_app Postgres role has INSERT and SELECT on evidence_records but no UPDATE or DELETE grants. Evidence is also Vault Transit-signed — any modification invalidates the cryptographic proof.

data.name: Vault Transit
data.description: Cryptographic operations boundary — signing, verification, hashing, certificate issuance. Private keys created with exportable=false. Vault audit backend logs all operations.

threats.title: Vault sealed during signing request
threats.description: If Vault is sealed (pod restart without auto-unseal, or Raft quorum loss), all signing operations fail and workflows stall at signing activities
threats.mitigation: Vault HA (3-node Raft) in staging/prod. Auto-unseal via cloud KMS. Temporal retry policy (10 attempts, exponential backoff) handles brief outages transparently. Chaos test verifies graceful failure behavior.

threats.title: Private key extraction via compromised Vault token
threats.description: Attacker with a stolen Vault AppRole token attempts to extract the raw private key material via the Transit export API
threats.mitigation: Transit keys created with exportable=false. The export API returns 403 regardless of token policy. Vault audit log records the failed attempt.

data.name: Keycloak
data.description: Identity provider and authorization server. Manages realms (one per tenant), clients, users, and service accounts. Issues short-lived JWTs.

threats.title: Keycloak Admin REST exposed to unauthorized services
threats.description: If the Keycloak Admin REST API is accessible to any pod in the cluster, a compromised pod could create new admin users or disable MFA
threats.mitigation: Kubernetes NetworkPolicy restricts Keycloak Admin REST (port 8080/auth/admin) to provisioning-agent pod only. All other platform pods cannot reach the admin path.
